body:
# The PassionFit Framework Map

The spine of the concepts brain: which families exist, which concepts belong to each, and how
everything else (verbatims, diagrams, seminar segments, source chunks) hangs off them. The
structured taxonomy lives in this record's frontmatter (`taxonomy` jsonb) — the /m/pf-concepts page
renders its families FROM that structure, so reorganising the taxonomy here reorganises the page.

> ⚠️ **Write-path fence (permanent):** after creation, this record's body is edited only via
> safe-path 1 (partial upsert) and its `taxonomy` only via safe-path 2 (whole-frontmatter jsonb
> merge). NEVER re-save it from a file — the file parser drops nested structures and would silently
> wipe the taxonomy the page renders from. Write-path rules live in [[pf-corpus-definition]].

## The families

1. **Philosophy Foundation Models** — how we think, frame the journey, and measure progression:
   [[potential|Potential]] · [[ipsative-assessment|Ipsative Assessment]] ·
   [[ipsative-progression-curve-green-line|Ipsative Progression Curve / Green Line]] ·
   [[high-functioning-matrix|High Functioning Matrix]] ·
   [[impact-influence-control-legacy|Impact – Influence – Control – Legacy]] ·
   [[the-development-paradox|The Development Paradox]]
2. **The Four Core Behaviours (Core Accomplishment, ECPC – "Easy Peasy")** — the behavioural core:
   [[effective-goal-setting|Effective Goal Setting]] · [[commitment-continuum|Commitment Continuum]] ·
   [[prioritisation|Prioritisation]] · [[control-the-controllables|Control the Controllables]]
3. **Coachability Behaviours** — what determines how effectively you can be coached:
   [[direction-support-matrix|Direction / Support Matrix]] ·
   [[intuition-scale-learning-behaviours|Intuition Scale & Learning Behaviours]] ·
   [[transactional-state|Transactional State]]
4. **Supporting Concepts** — the toolkit around the core:
   [[blame-and-ownership|Blame and Ownership]] · [[communication-hierarchy|Communication Hierarchy]] ·
   [[listening-behaviours|Listening Behaviours]] ·
   [[presence|Presence]] · [[safe-space-vs-soft-space|Safe Space vs Soft Space]] ·
   [[the-behaviours-of-the-accomplished|The Behaviours of the Accomplished]] ·
   [[seven-steps-of-performance|The Seven Steps of Performance]]
5. **Influences** — concepts, models and books Tom believes in but did not create (Johari Window,
   Viktor Frankl, Jonathan Livingston Seagull, Alan Watkins, Stephen Covey, …). Populated by the
   Influences library (execution-plan Stage D); each influence states what it is, who created it,
   and how Tom uses it.

Family hubs: [[category-philosophy-foundation|Philosophy Foundation overview]] ·
[[category-core-accomplishment|Core Accomplishment overview]] ·
[[category-coachability|Coachability overview]] · a Supporting Concepts overview (created in the
linking pass — it did not exist before) · an Influences overview (created with the library).
Cross-family references: [[glossary|The PassionFit Glossary]] · [[lessons-from-tom|Lessons from Tom]].

## How children attach

Support records (Tom's verbatims, diagrams, seminar segments, book/doc chunks) attach to a concept
by carrying its **concept-key tag** (e.g. `commitment-continuum`) and, after the linking pass, an
explicit "Maps to:" link. ~104 support records already carry concept keys; the remainder are placed
by the linking pass (embedding similarity proposes, the pass writes) or listed as unplaced.

## Dispositions of the 23 previously-unclassified concept-type records (Stage B)

- **22 "Tom in his own words" verbatim records → SUPPORT** (children of their concepts via
  concept-key tags). Nine of them carry concept keys with **no canonical concept page yet**:
  applied-coaching-science, coaching-philosophy, human-needs, self-sabotage, provisional-state,
  vuca-acuve, intuition-and-belief, practical-coaching-cues, stories-analogies. These attach to
  their nearest family hub and sit on the **unplaced-for-Pete list** below.
- **The Seven Steps of Performance (Mehmet, 2007) → PROMOTED to canonical** in Supporting Concepts
  (it is written as a proper concept page and already lives in the portal folder). ⚠️ Judgement
  call for Pete: it is externally authored, so it ALSO appears in the Influences sweep — if you'd
  rather it live as an Influence than a Supporting Concept, say so and it moves.

## Unplaced — for Pete's review (no silent catch-all)

Nine verbatim concept-keys have no canonical concept page: **human needs · self-sabotage ·
provisional state · VUCA/ACUVE · intuition and belief · coaching philosophy · applied coaching
science · practical coaching cues · stories & analogies**. Options per key: promote to a canonical
concept page later, or leave as family-hub-attached verbatim material (current state). Nothing is
lost either way — they are fully searchable and linked.

## Judgement log

- 2026-07-18: Taxonomy derived from the live family tags + category overviews + record content and
  encoded here; Pete authorised full execution ("execute 1-4 in full") — flag any family/member
  correction and it is a one-line jsonb fix that re-renders the page.

Power data is inconsistent between the home turbo trainer and the Wattbike, which will require careful management of training zones across different equipment.

It is unresolved who is at fault for the client's "pool life" issue.

The specific nature of the "scimitar things" that need to be actioned is unclear.

Action Items

The coach will review the athlete's training plan and add different types of runs as needed to ensure there is sufficient contrast.

Send the warm-up template for the 20-minute FTP test.

Perform a 20-minute FTP test on the Wattbike to establish power zones and gather max heart rate data.

Add plyometric exercises to one gym session per week to build leg conditioning for running.

Look into booking a physiological performance test for September at Hartpury College, Bath University, or Loughborough University.

Ask Mike Daly for more details about his testing experience.

Complete the 20-minute training test on Thursday using the provided template.

Robbie to get the "scimitar things" in.

Create a checklist to use for pools before starting work.




AI Suggestion

AI has identified the following issues that were not concluded in the meeting or lack clear action items; please pay attention:

The incident concerning the client's "pool life" remains entirely unresolved. The root cause of the problem has not been determined, and responsibility has not been assigned. This lack of resolution exposes the organisation to potential liability and leaves a significant client-facing issue outstanding.

A preventative action to create a checklist for pools was agreed upon, but this task lacks an owner, scope, and deadline. It is unclear who is responsible for creating the checklist, what specific items it should contain to prevent future issues, and when it is due. This ambiguity puts the preventative measure at high risk of not being implemented.

A critical technical issue exists with inconsistent power data between the Wattbike and the athlete's home turbo trainer. It is unresolved how this discrepancy will be managed, which threatens the accuracy of the upcoming FTP test and all subsequent training zones. Without a clear strategy, the effectiveness of the cycling training programme is at risk.

An action item assigned to "Robbie to get the 'scimitar things' in" is completely undefined. The nature of the "scimitar things" and the specific action required are unknown, making the task impossible to complete without immediate clarification.

Date: 2025-07-01 01:45:04

Location: [Insert Location]

Attendees: [Insert Names]

Keywords: Swimming Technique, Freestyle, Body Position




Theme

This lecture, presented by Brenton, a swim coach based in Australia, addresses the identification and correction of common technical faults in freestyle swimming, particularly for triathletes and open-water swimmers globally. The coach analyses typical errors such as incorrect head position, wrist drop, and sinking legs, emphasising that these issues often stem from poor balance and inefficient breathing. The core message is that swimmers can greatly enhance their technique and reduce fatigue by understanding these fundamental principles and using self-analysis tools like video recording.




Key Takeaways

Lifting the head to breathe is a frequent mistake; keeping the head down is often the first and most important correction.

A slight wrist drop on hand entry is a technical flaw to watch for, though it may be considered slightly irrelevant for general swimmers.

Filming yourself swimming, even with a phone above the water, is highly effective for identifying and understanding your own technique errors.

Sinking legs are usually due to poor balance in the water, not weak kicking, despite common advice from coaches to "kick harder."

Inefficient breathing, such as taking very small inhales (where almost half the head is in the water) or looking up at the sky, disrupts balance and causes legs to sink.

Stiffness and tension in the body are counterproductive; swimmers should aim to be fluid and relaxed, embodying the Bruce Lee saying "be like water."

Feeling exhausted after swimming a short distance (e.g., 100 metres) typically indicates poor technique rather than a lack of fitness.

Systematically working through fundamental principles, such as the 5 core principles available in the membership, provides a structured path to improvement through specific drills and exercises.




Highlights

"I like the Bruce Lee saying of be like water..."

— Speaker: Brenton (Swim Coach), Source: Bruce Lee

"May you find light at all times. May you find joy at all times. May you find peace at all times."

— Speaker: Unknown




Chapter Topics




1. Common Freestyle Swimming Faults

Description: The lecture outlines several common technical errors made by swimmers, especially triathletes and open-water swimmers. These faults mainly concern body position, breathing, and limb mechanics, leading to inefficiency and fatigue.

Key Points:

Head Position: Lifting the head to breathe is a primary error; the head should stay down as the first thing to change.

Wrist Position: Dropping the wrist as the hand enters the water is a subtle but important flaw, though the coach notes it might be "slightly irrelevant" for the general audience.

Sinking Legs: Legs dropping and creating drag is a widespread problem and a big reason why swimmers sink, often misattributed to poor kicking.

Poor Balance: The main cause of sinking legs is usually a lack of balance in the water.

Breathing: Taking very small inhales (where almost half the head is in the water) or looking up at the sky instead of to the side disrupts alignment and balance.

Tension: Swimming with a stiff, rigid body increases fatigue and reduces efficiency, contrasting with the desired fluid motion.

Examples:

Detail: The coach analyses a video of a swimmer named Bridge, observing that as Bridge reaches forward, he lifts his head to breathe and slightly drops his wrist as his hand extends.

Explanation: The coach identifies the head lift as the first issue to address, recommending keeping the head down. The wrist drop is noted as a secondary, subtler problem, which the coach suggests is "probably slightly irrelevant" for the general viewer. This example demonstrates the value of video analysis for spotting specific technical flaws.




2. The Connection Between Balance, Breathing, and Sinking Legs

Description: This section explains the relationship between breathing technique, body balance, and the common issue of sinking legs, challenging the notion that kicking harder is the solution.

Key Points:

Poor balance is the main reason legs sink in the water.

Breathing technique significantly affects overall body balance.

Taking very small inhales or looking up while breathing disrupts body alignment and balance.

When balance is compromised, the body is not streamlined, causing the legs to drop and create drag.

Coaches who advise swimmers to "kick harder" are often treating the symptom rather than the root cause. The real solution is to fix balance, starting with proper breathing and head position.




Assignments and Suggestions

If possible, have someone film you swimming, even with a phone from above the water, to better understand your technique and identify errors.

Watch the "Feedback Fridays" video playlist to gain a great sense of what to do and what to avoid in swimming technique.

If you are following the "5 core principles" in the membership, work through them one at a time, utilising the provided drills and exercises to ensure each element is correctly in place and to tick them off as you progress.

Subscribe to the Effortless Swimming channel and share the videos with others who are trying to improve their swimming, as this would be hugely helpful in spreading awareness.

Overview

This document consolidates and organises a detailed discussion focused on developing and refining a sponsorship strategy for an athlete, with future plans to benefit a wider team. The content covers outreach structuring, setting measurable targets, personalising communications, expanding channels, refining sponsorship packages, gathering sponsor feedback, enhancing storytelling, community engagement, sponsor retention, and practical tools. Key risks and open issues are highlighted, and all actionable tasks are grouped at the end for clarity and follow-up.

Sponsorship Outreach and Strategy

The immediate sponsorship efforts are for the individual, with the intention to allocate a percentage to a future team fund once the team is established.

There is a need to avoid delays in seeking sponsorship, even though the team initiative is not fully launched, to prevent wasting time and ensure progress.

Both participants agreed on the importance of clear communication regarding roles and responsibilities in the sponsorship process to avoid duplicated efforts, with the speaker offering to assist in outreach by speaking in the third person.

The immediate priority is to define clear numerical targets for outreach, focusing on activities rather than just monetary outcomes.

Example targets discussed included: five social media posts per month, ten sponsorship-seeking emails per month, and ten LinkedIn direct messages per month.

The rationale is that consistent outreach, by "knocking on more doors," increases the likelihood of securing sponsorships and provides a clear record of effort, regardless of immediate financial results. This structured approach is believed to eventually lead to financial success.

Targets should be realistic and scalable, starting small (e.g., five emails) and increasing as processes become more established and confidence grows.

Accountability between participants was emphasised, with a suggestion to divide outreach tasks (e.g., emails, messages) between them on a weekly or monthly basis, ensuring mutual support and shared responsibility.
